activities.title: Primary Assessment Practice
safetyNotes: Use a simulated patient only. Do not practice on an actually injured person.
Do not perform rescue breathing, chest compressions, or cervical-spine movement during this classroom simulation.
Stop the activity immediately if any participant feels uncomfortable.
instructions: Work in groups of three: responder, simulated patient, and observer.
The observer gives the responder a scenario without revealing the expected response.
The responder checks scene safety, uses appropriate protective equipment, assesses responsiveness, directs someone to activate the emergency plan, and identifies immediate life threats.
The responder clearly explains what should happen next without performing invasive care.
Rotate roles until every student has served as responder.
checklist: Confirms the scene is safe before approaching
Uses or verbalizes standard precautions
Checks responsiveness and breathing
Assigns a specific person to activate the emergency response plan
Identifies when emergency medical services and an AED are needed
Avoids unnecessary movement of the simulated patient
Communicates calmly and clearly
activities.writtenResponse: In 3–5 sentences, explain which primary-assessment step is easiest to skip under pressure and how a responder can prevent that mistake.

rubric.proficient: Checks scene safety and uses or verbalizes appropriate precautions before contact.
Completes the primary assessment in a logical order without skipping a critical step.
Gives a clear, specific direction to activate the emergency plan and obtain needed equipment.
Uses calm, concise language appropriate for the simulated emergency.

activities.title: Emergency Action Plan Walk-Through
safetyNotes: Do not call 911 or activate a real alarm during the practice.
Do not block entrances, hallways, or emergency-access routes.
Use only teacher-approved locations and contact information.
instructions: Choose one athletic venue used by your school or program.
Identify the exact venue address, the best emergency entrance, and the route an ambulance would use.
Identify who calls emergency medical services, who retrieves the AED, who meets responders, and who controls the scene.
Locate the nearest AED and emergency communication device or describe where they should be placed.
Walk through a simulated emergency verbally from the first recognition of danger until care is transferred to emergency responders.
Revise the plan after the walk-through to correct any unclear role, route, or communication step.
checklist: Venue name and exact address are recorded
Emergency entrance and responder route are identified
Emergency communication method is identified
AED location is identified
Four response roles are assigned
A person is assigned to meet and direct emergency responders
The plan includes crowd and scene control
The group identifies at least one weakness and revision
activities.writtenResponse: Describe the most likely delay in your venue’s emergency response and one realistic change that would reduce that delay.

rubric.proficient: Provides accurate location, access, communication, and equipment information.
Assigns clear, non-overlapping responsibilities to specific responders.
Walk-through follows a realistic sequence from recognition through transfer of care.
Identifies a meaningful weakness and proposes a workable improvement.

activities.title: Ankle Taping Decision Station
safetyNotes: Practice only on an uninjured, consenting classmate or a mannequin under teacher supervision.
Stop immediately for numbness, tingling, discoloration, pain, skin irritation, or discomfort.
Do not tape a suspected fracture, open wound, acute injury, or anyone with current pain.
This station is classroom practice and does not authorize independent clinical care.
instructions: Review the scenario and decide whether taping is appropriate or contraindicated.
Obtain consent, inspect the skin, and position the foot in a comfortable neutral position.
Verbally identify the purpose of anchors, support strips, heel locks, and closure strips before application.
Apply only the teacher-approved sequence with smooth, overlapping strips and no excessive tension.
Recheck circulation, sensation, comfort, and skin color after application.
Remove the tape using the teacher-approved method and inspect the skin again.
Clean the station and dispose of used materials appropriately.
checklist: Screens for contraindications before taping
Obtains consent and checks the skin
Positions the foot appropriately
Explains the purpose of each major tape component
Uses smooth strips without wrinkles or excessive tension
Rechecks circulation, sensation, comfort, and skin color
Removes tape safely and reinspects the skin
States when taping should be stopped or avoided
activities.writtenResponse: Explain two findings that would make you stop or avoid taping and describe the safest next step for each finding.

rubric.proficient: Correctly identifies when taping is appropriate and when referral or stopping is required.
Obtains consent, checks the skin, and positions the foot before application.
Uses the approved sequence with smooth strips and safe tension.
Rechecks circulation, sensation, comfort, and skin condition after taping.

activities.title: Simulated Splinting Station
safetyNotes: Use a simulated injury only and do not practice on anyone with current pain or injury.
Do not forcefully realign, apply traction, or move a simulated deformity.
Do not tighten a splint around an actual injury.
Classroom completion does not authorize independent care.
instructions: Read the scenario and verbalize scene safety and the need for adult or emergency assistance.
Ask the simulated patient to remain still and support the body part in the position found.
Verbally assess distal circulation, sensation, and movement without provoking pain.
Select and pad an appropriate practice splint.
Secure the splint above and below the simulated injury without covering the distal check area.
Reassess distal circulation, sensation, comfort, and movement after application.
State the referral or emergency decision and how the athlete would be monitored until help arrives.
checklist: Verbalizes scene safety and adult or emergency activation
Prevents unnecessary movement
Checks distal circulation, sensation, and movement before splinting
Chooses and pads an appropriate splint
Secures above and below the simulated injury
Avoids forceful realignment or traction
Repeats distal checks after application
States an appropriate referral or emergency plan
activities.writtenResponse: Explain why distal circulation, sensation, and movement are checked before and after splinting, and identify one change that would require loosening or removing the practice splint.

rubric.proficient: Prevents movement, avoids realignment, and verbalizes appropriate emergency support.
Performs and explains pre- and post-application circulation, sensation, and movement checks.
Selects, pads, and positions an appropriate practice splint.
Secures safely above and below the simulated injury and reassesses afterward.

activities.title: Sweat-Rate Calculation Lab
safetyNotes: Use teacher-provided or hypothetical data unless a supervised class protocol has been approved.
Do not use this activity to prescribe fluid intake or diagnose dehydration.
Hydration plans should follow qualified coaching, athletic-training, medical, and school guidance.
instructions: Record pre-exercise body mass, post-exercise body mass, fluid consumed, urine produced, and exercise duration.
Convert body-mass change and fluid volumes into the same unit before calculating.
Calculate sweat loss as pre-exercise mass minus post-exercise mass plus fluid consumed minus urine produced.
Convert the session sweat loss to an hourly rate using the exercise duration.
Compare the result with the provided example and check whether the answer is physically reasonable.
Write a cautious hydration-planning response that identifies what additional information a qualified adult would need.
checklist: All five required inputs are recorded
Mass and fluid values are converted to compatible units
Sweat-loss calculation uses the correct signs
Exercise duration is converted to hours
Hourly sweat rate is calculated correctly
Result is checked for reasonableness
Interpretation avoids making a medical prescription
activities.writtenResponse: Using the calculated sweat rate, explain two factors that a qualified coach, athletic trainer, or medical professional should consider before adjusting an athlete’s hydration plan.

rubric.proficient: Records all inputs and converts them into compatible units.
Calculates session sweat loss and hourly sweat rate accurately.
Checks the result and identifies an obvious unit or sign error if present.
Uses cautious language and identifies relevant contextual factors without prescribing care.

activities.title: Concussion Sideline-Card Roleplay
safetyNotes: Use roleplay only. Do not provoke symptoms or test balance on a potentially injured person.
Do not perform a real diagnostic examination during this simulation.
Students do not clear athletes to return to participation.
Do not place a real emergency call during the simulation.
instructions: Assign the roles of athlete, student observer, coach or teacher, and recorder.
Read a teacher-approved scenario card and identify observable signs, reported symptoms, and concerning mechanism information.
The observer states that the athlete should be removed from participation and informs the designated adult.
The recorder documents only the information provided in the scenario without adding a diagnosis.
The group decides whether the scenario requires routine adult follow-up or emergency escalation and explains why.
Rotate roles and repeat with a second scenario.
End each scenario by stating that return-to-play decisions belong to qualified healthcare professionals under applicable rules.
checklist: Recognizes at least one concerning sign or symptom
Removes the athlete from participation in the roleplay
Notifies a responsible adult
Documents observations without diagnosing
Identifies red flags that require emergency escalation
Does not perform symptom provocation or unsafe testing
Does not allow student-directed return to play
Communicates calmly and respectfully
activities.writtenResponse: Explain why a student observer should document what was seen and reported without diagnosing the athlete, and identify one red flag that requires immediate adult or emergency action.

rubric.proficient: Identifies relevant signs, symptoms, or mechanism information from the scenario.
Removes the athlete from participation and prevents student-directed return to play.
Notifies the appropriate adult and communicates observations without diagnosing.
Correctly distinguishes routine follow-up from emergency red-flag escalation.
